title: Lesson 29 — The Orator

words.definition: fluent and clear in expressing oneself
words.example: An articulate spokesperson calmed the anxious crowd.

words.definition: the art of formal, persuasive public speaking
words.example: Her stirring oratory won over even the doubters.

words.definition: sharp, vigorous, and incisive in expression
words.example: His trenchant analysis exposed the plan's flaws at once.
One incisive question unraveled the whole alibi.

words.definition: to shock or stir someone into sudden action
words.example: The speech galvanized volunteers across the country.

words.definition: to stir up feeling or provoke to action
words.example: Her words roused the weary crowd to its feet.
No argument could sway the stubborn jury.

words.definition: to strongly urge or encourage someone to do something
words.example: The coach exhorted the team to fight to the final whistle.
She made an impassioned plea for the shelter's funding.
The reformers pursued their cause with religious fervor.
The lawyer laid out a compelling case for acquittal.

words.definition: a leader who wins support by appealing to emotions and prejudice rather than reason
words.example: The demagogue stoked fear instead of offering solutions.

story: The square filled to hear the challenger speak. [[articulate|Articulate]] and poised, she was a master of [[oratory|oratory]], and her [[trenchant|trenchant]], [[incisive|incisive]] attacks on the mayor seemed to [[galvanize|galvanize]] the crowd. She could [[rouse|rouse]] a tired audience and [[sway|sway]] the undecided, and she would [[exhort|exhort]] them, in an [[impassioned|impassioned]] voice trembling with [[fervor|fervor]], to demand better. Her case was [[compelling|compelling]] — yet the old reporter in the back wondered whether so gifted a speaker was a true reformer or merely a [[demagogue|demagogue]].
